metric_value: Content management market: $1.2B (2002), $1.6B (2003 projected), $2.0B (2004 projected)

metric_value: EII projected to grow to $250M+ market within 2 years if major hardware/database suppliers successfully enter market
notes: Summary of Findings; conditional on IBM, BEA, Business Objects success

metric_value: EII to become key technology in $7.5 billion market as incorporated in portal, EAI, BI, and BPI solutions
notes: Combined impact across related markets

metric_value: Database administration costs now dominate TCO of applications below 500-user level and continue to increase in importance for all sizes
notes: Aberdeen research finding; EII positioned as solution for cross-database administration cost

metric_value: Slight web site disadvantage can cause 10-20% of installed base to walk away even in early e-commerce era
notes: Aberdeen research motivation for EII as competitive differentiator

metric_value: For every $1 spent on EII software licenses, $1.25 to $1.50 spent on professional services for implementation
notes: Aberdeen field experience estimate

metric_value: MetaMatrix is Cadillac of EII solutions — comprehensive features, developer/admin support, sophisticated cross-database application development
notes: Aberdeen Conclusions for MetaMatrix profile

metric_value: MetaMatrix announced OEM agreement with SAP to provide MetaMatrix Server embedded in SAP NetWeaver (Enterprise Services Architecture)
notes: Significant partnership bridging EII to SAP's ESA strategy

metric_value: EII is now a market separate from EAI and ETL; distinction driven by need to integrate information across enterprise as strategic infrastructure decision
notes: Preface: key market positioning claim

metric_value: Business benefit of EII increases exponentially with successive integration of each related application and database
notes: Summary of Findings; network effects of integration

metric_value: Aberdeen expects all EII suppliers to wrap transactions in XML within next 2 years (by 2005)
notes: Standardization and Integration section; XML as key future standard

metric_value: Most M&A activity over next 2 years will center on larger infrastructure solution providers acquiring newcomer EII suppliers
notes: Major Forces: M&A section; IBM and BEA quickest to respond

metric_value: IBM and BEA quickest to respond to EII opportunity; Oracle and Microsoft have not shown full appreciation of EII potential
notes: Technology Interdependency section

metric_value: EII solutions provide specific value-add to enterprise portal upgrades by enabling cross-database data and transactions, simplifying developer job
notes: Aberdeen Observations and Conclusions

metric_value: Web-servicized EII solution can provide easier access by other Web services to local, remote, and cross-company data
notes: Aberdeen Observations: EII can play important role in Web services implementations

metric_value: Distinct EII market exists, beginning 'high-end user per-project' stage; driven by need to rationalize multiple database access to cut admin costs and leverage cross-database proprietary information
notes: Aberdeen Observations and Conclusions

metric_value: No single supplier dominates EII market; most EII suppliers are small companies 1-4 years old; market awareness began 2001
notes: Distribution and Implementation section

metric_value: Sybase open veneer provides common SQL front-end to its own and other relational databases; lets developers write to common SQL; does not allow cross-supplier transactions
notes: Database Technologies: Open Veneers section

metric_value: Wide array of ETL tools from Compuware, Data Junction, ETI, Sybase, TIBCO, and webMethods; applicable as base technology for wide variety of information aggregation needs
notes: Replication and ETL Tools section

metric_value: All major enterprise database suppliers offer extensive data warehousing support: IBM, Microsoft, Oracle, Sybase, plus NCR-Teradata
notes: Data Warehouses and Data Marts section

metric_value: Idea of enterprise operational data store pioneered by Unisys; now generally accepted across industry
notes: Enterprise Operational Data Store section

metric_value: Vision of one enterprise-standard database or federated database encompassing all data sources is 'pretty much dead'; data migration of all legacy databases too hard for most enterprises
notes: Federated Database section; Informix federated approach noted

metric_value: EII visibility grown dramatically over last year, especially due to IBM's announcement of Information Integrator products
notes: Summary of Findings; IBM as EII market catalyst

metric_value: Six enabling technologies for EII in 2003: application servers, Java, XML, database administration technologies, enterprise portals, and Web services
notes: The Future section; all critical for long-term EII value

metric_value: Aberdeen EII Technology Segmentation Model: EAI technology (30+ vendors), Database technology (IBM/Microsoft/Oracle/Sybase), XML messaging (20+ vendors), EII solution (Attunity/Business Objects/Ipedo/XAware/MetaMatrix/Venetica/BEA/Nimble/IBM)
notes: Table 6 and 7: EII and Related Suppliers; Aberdeen segmentation model

metric_value: EII typically lacks persistence technology; combination of EII and ODS is better than either separately for data-store size and performance; EII/mid-tier ODS enables 'have it your way' architecture
notes: Usefulness of Combining Mid-tier ODS and EII section

metric_value: IT buyers should assess existing information aggregation tools, supplement where necessary, create long-range plan to use EII as ongoing effort to amass metadata about all enterprise data sources
notes: Action Items section; political payoff of data fiefdoms joining EII effort

metric_value: Suppliers should implement full-fledged information aggregation tool set combining data migration, replication/ETL, data warehouse, ODS, and EII tools with guidance on when to use each
notes: Action Items: no one has combined these tools in overall tool set yet

metric_value: Many EII suppliers have experience in data integration and data warehouse ETL; ETL tools recently appeared in offerings of EII suppliers
notes: Summary of Findings; ETL providers (Compuware, Data Junction, ETI, Sybase, TIBCO, webMethods) relevant to EII

metric_value: Partial deployment of intranet/extranet/internet applications without EII increases difficulties of implementing EII and increases costs of failing to implement EII
notes: Summary of Findings; path dependency argument for early EII adoption

metric_value: EII enables three high-priority e-business initiatives: CRM, e-business integration across supply chain, and web-based employee productivity applications
notes: Summary of Findings; core business value proposition

metric_value: Progress Corp: ObjectStore XML database (mid-tier ODS), PeerDirect InnerEdge/OuterEdge replication (ETL/migration), SonicMQ XML messaging, OpenEdge embedded infrastructure — combination covers multiple EII-related categories
notes: Combinations of Technologies section; example of technology combination

metric_value: EII today is project-specific, seeking to simplify developer interfaces; portals, web services making cross-database queries as initial EII implementations
notes: Summary of Findings: initial EII implementations are project specific

metric_value: EII will form part of larger information aggregation market delivering tools for strategic information management over next 2 years
notes: Summary of Findings; EII evolving from standalone tool to ecosystem component

metric_value: EII can serve as focal point for gathering unprecedented amount of metadata about enterprise proprietary information; potential for cross-data-source administration
notes: EII Tools section; not yet on users radar screens per Aberdeen

metric_value: EII suppliers establishing alliances with Big Five PSOs (Andersen Consulting, Deloitte & Touche, Ernst & Young, KPMG, PricewaterhouseCoopers) and HP and Sun
notes: Distribution and Implementation section; services-led model emerging
